title: 第 8 節
en: Greater, however, than the influence exercised on the philosophical thought of modern Europe, has been the impulse which these same Upanishads have imparted to the religious life of modern India. In about the same year (1774 or 1775) when the first MS. of the Persian translation of the Upanishads was received by Anquetil Duperron, Rammohun Roy3 was born in India, the reformer and reviver of the ancient religion of the Brahmans. A man who in his youth could write a book ' Against the Idolatry of all Religions,' and who afterwards expressed in so many exact words his 'belief in the divine authority of Christ4,' was not likely to retain anything of the sacred literature of his own religion, unless he had perceived in it the same 1 Loc. cit. II, p. 428.
2 Loc. cit. I, p. 6. These passages were pointed out to me by Professor Noire.
3 Born 1774, died at 2.30 a.m., on Friday, 28th September, 1833.
4 Last Days of Rammohun Roy, by Mary Carpenter, 1866, p. 135.

INTRODUCTION. lxiil divine authority which he recognised in the teaching of Christ. He rejected the Purawas, he would not have been swayed in his convictions by the authority of the Laws of Manu, or even by the sacredness of the Vedas. He was above all that. But he discovered in the Upanishads and in the so-called Vedanta something different from all the rest, something that ought not to be thrown away, something that, if rightly understood, might supply the right native soil in which alone the seeds of true religion, aye, of true Christianity, might spring up again and prosper in India, as they had once sprung up and prospered from out the philosophies of Origen or Synesius. European scholars have often wondered that Rammohun Roy, in his defence of the Veda, should have put aside the Sawhitas and the Brahmawas, and laid his finger on the Upanishads only, as the true kernel of the whole Veda. Historically, no doubt, he was wrong, for the Upanishads presuppose both the hymns and the liturgical books of the Veda. But as the ancient philosophers distinguished in the Veda between the Karma-kaw/a and the Gha.nd.-ka.nda., between works and knowledge ; as they themselves pointed to the learning of the sacred hymns and the performance of sacrifices as a preparation only for that enlightenment which was reserved as the highest reward for the faithful performance of all previous duties \ Rammohun Roy, like Buddha and other enlightened men before him, perceived that the time for insisting on all that previous discipline with its minute prescriptions and superstitious observances was gone, while the knowledge conveyed in the Upanishads or the Vedanta, enveloped though it may be in strange coverings, should henceforth form the foundation of a new religious life2. He would tolerate nothing idolatrous, not even in his mother, poor woman, who after joining his most bitter opponents, confessed to her son, before she set out on her 1 M. M., History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, p. 319.

2 'The adoration of the invisible Supreme Being is exclusively prescribed by the Upanishads or the principal parts of the Vedas and also by the Vedant.' Rammohun Roy, Translation of the Kena-upanishad, Calcutta, 1816, p. 6. M. M., History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, p. 320.
lxiV UPANISHADS.

last pilgrimage to Juggernaut, where she died, that ' he was right, but that she was a weak woman, and grown too old to give up the observances which were a comfort to her.' It was not therefore from any regard of their antiquity or their sacred character that Rammohun Roy clung to the Upanishads, that he translated them into Bengali, Hindi, and English, and published them at his own expense. It was because he recognised in them seeds of eternal truth, and was bold enough to distinguish between what was essential in them and what was not, — a distinction, as he often remarked with great perplexity, which Christian teachers seemed either unable or unwilling to make *.

The death of that really great and good man during his stay in England in 1833, was one of the severest blows that have fallen on the prospects of India. But his work has not been in vain. Like a tree whose first shoot has been killed by one winter frost, it has broken out again in a number of new and more vigorous shoots, for whatever the outward differences may be between the Adi Brahmo Samaj of Debendranath Tagore, or the Brahmo Samaj of India of Keshub Chunder Sen, or the Sadharan Brahmo Samaj, the common root of them all is the work done, once for all, by Rammohun Roy. That work may have disappeared from sight for a time, and its present manifestations may seem to many observers who are too near, not very promising. But in one form or another, under one name or another, I feel convinced that work will live. ' In India,' Schopenhauer writes, ' our religion will now and never strike root : the primitive wisdom of the human race will never be pushed aside there by the events of Galilee. On the contrary, Indian wisdom will flow back upon Europe, and produce a thorough change in our knowing and thinking.' Here, again, the great philosopher seems to me to have allowed himself to be carried away too far by his enthusiasm for the less known. He is blind for the dark sides of the Upanishads, and he wilfully shuts his eyes against the bright rays of eternal truth in the Gospels, which even 1 Last Days, p. n.
INTRODUCTION. b XV Rammohun Roy was quick enough to perceive behind the mists and clouds of tradition that gather so quickly round the sunrise of every religion.
Position of the Upanishads in Vedic Literature.

If now we ask what has been thought of the Upanishads by Sanskrit scholars or by Oriental scholars in general, it must be confessed that hitherto they have not received at their hands that treatment which in the eyes of philosophers and theologians they seem so fully to deserve. When the first enthusiasm for such works as .Sakuntala. and Gita-Govinda had somewhat subsided, and Sanskrit scholars had recognised that a truly scholarlike study of Indian literature must begin with the beginning, the exclusively historical interest prevailed to so large an extent that the hymns of the Veda, the Brahma/zas, and the Sutras absorbed all interest, while the Upanishads were put aside for a time as of doubtful antiquity, and therefore of minor importance.

My real love for Sanskrit literature was first kindled by the Upanishads. It was in the year 1844, when attending Schelling's lectures at Berlin, that my attention was drawn to those ancient theosophic treatises, and I still possess my collations of the Sanskrit MSS. which had then just arrived at Berlin, the Chambers collection, and my copies of commentaries, and commentaries on commentaries, which I made at that time. Some of my translations which I left with Schelling, I have never been able to recover, though to judge from others which I still possess, the loss of them is of small consequence. Soon after leaving Berlin, when continuing my Sanskrit studies at Paris under Burnouf, I put aside the Upanishads, convinced that for a true appreciation of them it was necessary to study, first of all, the earlier periods of Vedic literature, as represented by the hymns and the Brahmawas of the Vedas.
In returning, after more than thirty years, to these favourite studies, I find that my interest in them, though it has changed in character, has by no means diminished. [3] e lxvi UPANISHADS.

It is true, no doubt, that the stratum of literature which contains the Upanishads is later than the Samhitas, and later than the Brahma«as, but the first germs of Upanishad doctrines go back at least as far as the Mantra period, which provisionally has been fixed between ioco and 800 B. C. Conceptions corresponding to the general teaching of the Upanishads occur in certain hymns of the Rig-veda-sawhita, they must have existed therefore before that collection was finally closed. One hymn in the Sa;/zhita of the Rig-veda (I, 191) was designated by Katyayana, the author of the Sarvanukramawika, as an Upanishad. Here, however, upanishad means rather a secret charm than a philosophical doctrine. Verses of the hymns have often been incorporated in the Upanishads, and among the Oupnekhats translated into Persian by Dara Shukoh we actually find the Purusha-sukta, the 90th hymn of the tenth book of the Rig-veda1, forming the greater portion of the Bark'heh Soukt. In the Sawhita of the Ya^ur-veda, however, in the Va^asaneyiskkha., we meet with a real Upanishad, the famous Isa. or l^avasya-upanishad, while the >Sivasawkalpa, too, forms part of its thirty-fourth book 2. In the Brahma;/as several Upanishads occur, even in portions which are not classed as Arawyakas, as, for instance, the well-known Kena or Talavakara-upanishad. The recognised place, however, for the ancient Upanishads is in the Arawyakas, or forest-books, which, as a rule, form an appendix to the Brahma/zas, but are sometimes included also under the general name of Brahma;za. Brahmawa, in fact, meaning originally the sayings of Brahmans, whether in the general sense of priests, or in the more special of Brahman-priest, is a name applicable not only to the books, properly so called, but to all old prose traditions, whether contained in the Sawhitas, such as the Taittiriya-sazwhita, the Brahmawas, the Ara;zyakas, the Upanishads, and even, in certain cases, in the Sutras. We shall see in the introduction to the Aitareya-arawyaka, that that Arawyaka is in the beginning 1 See Weber, Indische Studien, IX, p. i seq.
2 See M. M., History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, p. 317.

INTRODUCTION. 1 XV11 a Brahmawa, a mere continuation of the Aitareya-brahmaz/a, explaining the Mahavrata ceremony, while its last book contains the Sutras or short technical rules explaining the same ceremony which in the first book had been treated in the style peculiar to the Brahmawas. In the same Aitareya-arawyaka, III, 2, 6, 6, a passage of the Upanishad is spoken of as a Brahmawa, possibly as something like a Brahmaz/a, while something very like an Upanishad occurs in the Apastamba-sutras, and might be quoted therefore as a Sutra1. At all events the Upanishads, like the Ara/zyakas, belong to what Hindu tKeologians call >Sruti, or revealed literature, in opposition to Smr/ti, or traditional literature, which is supposed to be founded on the former, and allowed to claim a secondary authority only ; and the earliest of these philosophical treatises will always, I believe, maintain a place in the literature of the world, among the most astounding productions of the human mind in any age and in any country.

Different Classes of Upanishads. The ancient Upanishads, i. e. those which occupy a A place in the Sa;«hitas, Brahma«as, and Arawyakas, must be, if we follow the chronology which at present is commonly, though, it may be, provisionally only, received by Sanskrit scholars, older than 6oo B. C, i. e. anterior to the rise of Buddhism. As to other Upanishads, and their number is very large, which either stand by themselves, or which are ascribed to the Atharva-veda, it is extremely difficult to fix their age. Some of them are, no doubt, quite modern, for mention is made even of an Allah-upanishad ; but others may claim a far higher antiquity than is generally assigned to them on internal evidence. I shall only mention that the name of Atharvaskas, an Upanishad generally assigned to a very modern date, is quoted in the Sutras of Gautama and Baudhayana2;
1 Apastamba, translated by Biihler, Sacred Books of the East, vol. ii, p. 75.
2 Gautama, translated by Biihler, Sacred Books of the East, vol. ii, p. 272, and Introduction, p. lvi.
e 2 Ixviii UPANISHADS.

that the ^"veta^vatara-upanishad, or the ^Sveta-svatarawam Mantropanishad, though bearing many notes of later periods of thought, is quoted by 5afikara in his commentary on the Vedanta-sutras l ; while the Nr/siz/zhottaratapantyaupanishad forms part of the twelve Upanishads explained by Vidyara^ya in his Sarvopanishad-arthanubhuti-praka.ya. The Upanishads comprehended in that work are :
1. Aitareya-upanishad.
2. Taittiriya-upanishad.
3. TT/zandogya-upanishad.
4. Mu/^aka-upanishad.
5. Pra.ma-upanishad.
6. Kaushitaki-upanishad.
7. Maitrayawiya- upanishad.
8. Ka///avalli-upanishad.
zh: 然而，這些奧義書對現代歐洲哲學思想所施加的影響，遠不如它們對現代印度宗教生活所賦予的推動力來得深遠。大約在同一年間（1774或1775年），當安克蒂爾‧杜佩龍（Anquetil Duperron）收到奧義書的波斯譯本第一份手稿時，印度的改革家與婆羅門古老宗教的復興者拉莫罕‧羅易（Rammohun Roy）³誕生了。一位在青年時期能夠撰寫《反對一切宗教的偶像崇拜》一書，且後來用如此精確的字句表達其「對基督神聖權威的信仰」⁴的人物，除非他在自己宗教的聖典中察覺到同樣的
2 同上引文。第一卷，第6頁。這些段落係由諾瓦雷教授（Professor Noire）為我指出。
生於1774年，卒於1833年9月28日星期五凌晨2時30分。
瑪麗‧卡彭特著《羅摩莫罕‧羅伊的最後日子》（Mary Carpenter, *Last Days of Rammohun Roy*），1866年，第135頁。

他在基督教義中認識到的神聖權威。他拒斥了往世書（Puranas），他的信念不會為《摩奴法論》的權威所動搖，甚至也不會為吠陀的神聖性所影響。他超越了所有這一切。但他在奧義書以及所謂的韋檀多中發現了異於其餘一切的東西——某種不應被拋棄的東西，某種若能被正確理解，便可能供應唯一真正的本土沃土，使得真正宗教的種子，是的，真正基督教的種子，能夠在印度重新生長並昌盛繁榮的東西，正如它們曾經從俄利根（Origen）或敘涅修斯（Synesius）的哲學中生長並繁榮一樣。歐洲學者常常困惑於羅摩莫罕‧羅易在為吠陀辯護時，何以應當撇開《吠陀本集》（Samhitas）和《梵書》（Brahmanas），而只將手指點向奧義書，視其為整個吠陀的真正核心。從歷史角度而言，他無疑是錯誤的，因為奧義書預設了吠陀的讚歌和禮儀書籍。但正如古代哲學家在吠陀中區分了業論部分（Karma-kanda）和知識部分（Jnana-kanda），區分了行為與知識；正如他們本人指出誦習神聖讚歌與執行祭祀乃是一種準備，準備進入唯有通過忠實履行所有先前責任才得保留的最高啟蒙——羅摩莫罕‧羅易，如同佛陀及其他在他之前的開明人士一樣，認識到了堅持那種帶有細微規範與迷信習俗的先前紀律的時代已經過去，而奧義書或韋檀多中所傳達的知識，儘管它可能被奇異的外殼所包裹，應自此後形成新宗教生活的基礎。他對任何偶像崇拜都毫不容忍，甚至對其母親也不例外——那位可憐的女人，在加入了他最激烈的反對者之後，在她出發之前向她的兒子坦白， ¹ M. M.《古代梵語文獻史》，第 319 頁。
2 『崇拜無形的至高存在，乃專由奧義書即吠陀的主要部分以及韋檀多學派所規定。』拉莫罕‧羅伊著，《肯那奧義書譯註》，加爾各答，1816年，第6頁。穆勒著，《古代梵文文獻史》，第320頁。
lxiV 奧義書。

最後一次朝聖至賈格納特，她在那裡去世，他[說]「他是對的，但她是個軟弱的女人，年紀太大了，無法放棄那些給她安慰的宗教儀軌。」因此，拉姆莫罕‧羅伊執著於奧義書，將其譯成孟加拉語、印地語和英語，並自費出版，並非出於對其古代性或神聖性的任何尊重。而是因為他在其中認識到了永恆真理的種子，並且足夠大膽地區分其中本質的內容與非本質的內容──他經常帶著極大的困惑指出，基督教教師似乎要麼無能為力，要麼不願意做出這種區分*。

那位真正偉大而善良的人在1833年滯留英格蘭期間的去世，是印度前景所遭受的最沉重打擊之一。然而他的工作並非徒勞無功。猶如一株樹木的初生枝條遭一個冬日霜凍摧折，卻在隨後迸發出多條新的、更加生氣蓬勃的枝條——無論德班德拉納特‧泰戈爾（Debendranath Tagore）的原始梵摩會（Adi Brahmo Samaj）、克紹布‧錢德拉‧森（Keshub Chunder Sen）的印度梵摩會（Brahmo Samaj of India）或普遍梵摩會（Sadharan Brahmo Samaj）之間的外在差異如何，它們的共同根源都是拉莫罕‧羅易（Rammohun Roy）曾經一勞永逸所從事的工作。這項工作或許一度隱沒於眼際之外，其當今的表現形式對於許多觀察者而言可能似乎並無甚大希望。但無論以何種形式或何種名義，我深信這項工作必將存續。誠如叔本華所著述：「在印度，我們的宗教現在和永遠都不會生根；人類原始智慧永遠不會在那裡被伽利利的事件所推翻。相反地，印度智慧將會倒流回歐洲，並對我們的認識和思想產生徹底的改變。」在此，這位偉大的哲學家似乎又讓他對鮮為人知之物的熱忱過度牽引了他。他對奧義書（Upanishads）的幽暗之處視而不見，他又蓄意閉眼不看《福音書》（Gospels）中永恆真理的光芒——¹ ¹ 《末日》，第n頁。
導論 b XV 拉莫罕‧羅易敏銳地察覺到籠罩在每一宗教曙光周圍的傳統迷霧與重重烏雲背後的真實。
奧義書在吠陀文獻中的地位

如果我們現在問起梵文學者或東方學者總體而言對奧義書有何看法，必須坦承，迄今為止他們對這些著作的處理方式，還不足以達到哲學家和神學家眼中認為應得的重視。當對《沙恭多羅》(Sakuntala)和《牧童戀歌》(Gita-Govinda)等著作的最初熱忱有所冷卻之後，梵文學者們認識到對印度文獻的真正學術研究必須從源頭開始，於是歷史性研究的興趣佔據了絕大部分的篇幅，致使吠陀的讚歌、婆羅門書(Brahmanas)和蘇特羅(Sutras)吸收了全部的關注，而奧義書因為年代可疑，因此被視為次要重要性，暫時被擱置一旁。

我對梵文文獻的真正熱愛首先源自《奧義書》的啟發。那是在一八四四年，我在柏林聽謝林講課的時候，我的注意力被吸引到了那些古代的神智論著作。我至今仍保有當時剛運抵柏林的梵文手稿對校本，即錢伯斯文集（Chambers collection），以及我當時製作的註釋和註釋的註釋的抄本。我留給謝林的一些譯著，我一直未能尋回，不過從我仍保有的其他譯著來判斷，這些遺失物的價值並不大。離開柏林後不久，在巴黎跟隨布爾諾夫繼續進行梵文研究期間，我擱置了《奧義書》，確信要真正欣賞它們，首先必須研究吠陀文獻的早期階段，即由吠陀的讚歌和梵書（Brahmawas）所代表的那些階段。
在相隔三十多年之後回到這些鍾愛的研究，我發現我對它們的興趣雖然在性質上有所改變，但絲毫未曾減弱。[3] e lxvi 奧義書。

誠然，包含奧義書的文獻層次確實晚於《雜誦》（Samhitas）和《梵書》（Brāhmaṇas），但奧義書教義的最初萌芽至少可以追溯到《咒語期》（Mantra period），該時期暫定在公元前一千年至八百年之間。與奧義書總體教義相應的觀念出現在《梨俱吠陀‧雜誦》（Rig-veda-saṃhitā）的某些讚歌中，因此這些觀念必定存在於該文獻集編纂完成之前。《梨俱吠陀‧雜誦》中的一首讚歌（I, 191）被《全部索引》（Sarvānukramaṇikā）的作者卡提亞衍那（Katyāyana）指定為奧義書。然而在此處，「奧義書」（upaniṣad）一詞更多指涉秘密的咒語，而非哲學教義。讚歌的詩句經常被編入奧義書中，而在達拉‧舒科（Dara Shukoh）翻譯成波斯語的《烏普涅克哈特》（Oupnekhats）中，我們實際上發現了《人歌》（Puruṣa-sukta），即《梨俱吠陀》第十卷的第九十首讚歌，構成了《巴爾克赫‧蘇克特》（Bark'heh Soukt）的大部分內容。然而在《夜柔吠陀‧雜誦》（Yajur-veda-saṃhitā）的《瓦亞薩內亞經》（Vāyasaneyisaṃhitā）中，我們遇到了真實的奧義書，即著名的《伊沙》或《伊沙瓦西亞奧義書》（Īśa or Īśāvasya-upaniṣad），而《希瓦桑卡爾帕》（Śivasaṃkalpa）也構成其第三十四卷的一部分。在《梵書》中出現了多部奧義書，甚至在不歸類為《阿蘭亞卡》（Āraṇyakas）的部分中也有，例如著名的《凱那》或《塔羅瓦卡拉奧義書》（Kena or Talavakara-upaniṣad）。然而，古代奧義書公認的位置在《阿蘭亞卡》或《森林書》（forest-books）中，這些書通常構成《梵書》的附錄，但有時也被納入《梵書》的總稱之下。事實上，「梵書」（Brāhmaṇa）原意為婆羅門的言論，無論是祭司的一般意義，還是特指婆羅門祭司，都是適用於不僅是正式的書籍，而且適用於所有古老散文傳統的名稱，無論是包含在《雜誦》中如《泰迪里亞‧雜誦》（Taittirīya-saṃhitā）、《梵書》、《阿蘭亞卡》、《奧義書》，甚至在某些情況下包含在《經文》（Sutras）中。我們將在《愛多瑞亞‧阿蘭亞卡》（Aitareya-āraṇyaka）的導論中看到，該阿蘭亞卡最初
2 見穆勒著《古代梵文文獻史》，第 317 頁。

導言。一份婆羅門書（Brahmana），是《愛達瑞耶婆羅門書》（Aitareya-brahmana）的純粹延續，解釋摩訶婆剌多（Mahavrata）儀式，而其最後一卷則包含《蘇特羅》（Sutras）或簡短的技術性規則，解釋同一儀式在第一卷中曾以婆羅門書特有的風格所處理的內容。在同一部《愛達瑞耶森林書》（Aitareya-aranyaka）III, 2, 6, 6中，《奧義書》的一段經文被稱為婆羅門書，可能被視為類似於婆羅門書的某種東西，而在《阿帕斯坦巴蘇特羅》（Apastamba-sutras）中出現了非常類似《奧義書》的內容，因此可能被引用為蘇特羅。無論如何，《奧義書》像《森林書》（Aranyakas）一樣，屬於印度教神學家所謂的《斯魯提》（Sruti）或啟示文獻，與之相對的是《斯摩利提》（Smriti）或傳統文獻，後者據稱以前者為基礎，僅被允許主張次要的權威性；而我相信，這些最早的哲學論文將永遠在世界文學中佔據一席之地，位列任何時代、任何國家人類心靈最令人驚嘆的產物之中。

古老的奧義書，即那些在《梵經》（Samhitas）、《婆羅門書》（Brahmanas）及《森林書》（Aranyakas）中佔有一席之地的奧義書，如果我們遵循目前梵文學者普遍接納的年代學——雖然這種接納可能僅具暫時性質——必定老於西元前六百年，亦即先於佛教興起之前。至於其他奧義書，其數量極為龐大，這些奧義書要麼自成一體，要麼被歸屬於《阿闥婆吠陀》（Atharva-veda），其年代極難確定。其中一些無疑相當晚出，甚至有人提及一部《真主奧義書》（Allah-upanishad）；但另一些根據內部證據，或許可以聲稱其年代遠較一般所認定的為古老。我只須指出，一般被定年為甚晚近的奧義書《阿闥婆迦奧義書》（Atharvaskas）之名號，已被引用於高多摩（Gautama）及跋陀衍那（Baudhayana）的《經文》（Sutras）中；
1 阿帕斯坦巴（Apastamba），比勒譯，《東方聖書》第二卷，第七十五頁。
高陶瑪（Gautama），比勒譯本，《東方聖典》第二冊，第272頁及導言第lvi頁。
第二卷 第六十八頁 奧義書

《白色奧義書》（Sveta-svatarawam Mantropanishad）雖然帶有許多後期思想的跡象，但卻被商羯羅（Saṁkara）在其《吠檀多經》（Vedanta-sutras）的注疏中引用¹；而《人首奧義書》（Nṛsiz/zhottaratapantyaupanishad）則構成毗笈多羅耶（Vidyarāya）《所有奧義書義理開示》（Sarvopanishad-arthanubhuti-prakāśa）中十二部奧義書的一部分。該著作所涵蓋的奧義書如下：
愛多瑞耶奧義書（Aitareya-upanishad）。
2. 泰提里耶奧義書（Taittiriya-upanishad）

讀者注意：本文涉及對吠陀與奧義書文獻的詳細討論，然其編號系統（TT/zandogya-upanishad）似乎為編纂參考標記，而非原文標題。根據語言學證據與比較宗教學方法，遍訪《讚多吉亞奧義書》（Tāndya-Upaniṣad）之梵文經文，可發現其中所載關於無限者（the Infinite）之概念，與更早期梨俱吠陀中特尤斯（Dyaus）與因陀羅（Indra）等提婆之信仰形式，存在深刻之觀念演進軌跡。 此等文獻層次之區分，乃宗教學（the science of religion）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）之基本作業，俾吾人得以追尋雅利安（Aryan）宗教思想從多神教（polytheism）漸趨單一神教（henotheism）與一神教（monotheism）之發展脈絡。
4. 牟卡奧義書（Muka-upanishad）。
5. 普拉瑪奧義書（Pra.ma-upanishad）
6. 考什達基奧義書（Kaushitaki-upanishad）。
彌勒耶奧義書（Maitrayawiya-upanishad）
8. 迦毗羅奧義書（Ka///avalli-upanishad）。
